narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which stands out above the typical 3.21 average, along with your LSAT score of 33, your upper-middle family income background, and your plan to attend full-time. Your Year 1 school decile ranking of 5 is close to the average of about 5.7, but your Year 3 decile of 3 falls notably below the typical 5.5. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 61.9% chance that you would fail it on your first try—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in practice. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on a few key aspects of your profile that carried the most weight in the decision. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 academic decile ranking, as being in the lower third of your school's performance that year signaled potential challenges in sustaining strong grades over time, which strongly correlates with bar exam outcomes. Next, your undergraduate GPA pulled in the opposite direction, helping to lower the failure risk because it's higher than average and shows solid foundational ability. Your LSAT score, however, contributed toward the failure prediction since 33 is below the dataset's average of 36.7, making it a notable drag despite your other strengths. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your Year 3 performance and LSAT align more closely with those who struggle, even as your GPA gives you an edge over the norm. This combination tipped the scales just enough for the model to flag higher risk. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above 37, strengthening your Year 3 record through additional coursework or explanations in a reapplications essay, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience as a Hispanic student overcoming any barriers. Reapplying next cycle with these improvements could shift your predicted odds significantly in your favor.
